sentences.base_language: I am studying abroad in Canada.
sentences.target_language: カナダに留学しています。
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ています (〜te imasu) can describe either an ongoing action (currently in progress) or a resulting state that continues. Here 留学しています means 'I am in a state of studying abroad' — a continuing situation, not just a momentary action.

sentences.base_language: I have been living here for three months.
sentences.target_language: もう三か月間ここに住んでいます。
sentences.romanized: Mō sankagetsu-kan koko ni sunde imasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**もう** (mō) = 'already / now (at this point)' — emphasizes that a state has been going on and continues. Compare: **まだ** (mada, still/not yet) is the counterpart for ongoing negatives.
**三か月間** (sankagetsu-kan) = 'for three months': **か月** (kagetsu) is the counter for months duration; **間** (kan) reinforces the sense of a time span.
**住んでいます** (sunde imasu) = 'am living / have been living': **住む** (sumu, to live/reside) → te-form **住んで** + **います** (ongoing state of residence).

sentences.base_language: Today I am feeling very homesick.
sentences.target_language: 今日はとてもホームシックです。
sentences.grammarNote:
**ホームシック** (hōmusikku) is a loanword from English 'homesick'. The **〜です** ending makes it a nominal predicate: 'I am (in a state of) homesickness'.
**今日は** uses **は** to set 'today' as the contrasting topic — implying perhaps that today is especially bad compared to other days.
**とても** (totemo) = 'very / extremely' — a common degree adverb for adjectives and noun predicates. Less formal than **非常に** (hijō ni, extremely).

〜たい (〜tai) attaches to the verb stem to express the speaker's desire: 食べたい (want to eat), 帰りたい (want to go home). The object of the wanted action often takes が or に. Important: 〜たい expresses your own desires — for someone else's desires, use 〜たがっている.

sentences.base_language: I miss my mother's home cooking.
sentences.target_language: お母さんの手料理が食べたいです。
sentences.romanized: Okāsan no teryōri ga tabetai desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 手料理 (teryōri) literally means 'hand-cooking' — food made by hand at home. The object of 〜たい takes が (not を), which is a common point of confusion: 〜が食べたい is more natural than 〜を食べたい when expressing personal craving.

sentences.base_language: I decide to call my mother.
sentences.target_language: お母さんに電話することにしました。
sentences.romanized: Okāsan ni denwa suru koto ni shimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことにする** (koto ni suru) = 'to decide to do ~': the nominalizer **こと** turns the verb into a noun phrase, and **にする** means 'to make it [that]'. This expresses a decision made by the speaker.
Compare: **〜ことになる** (koto ni naru) = 'it has been decided that' — passive/external decision vs **〜ことにする** — active personal decision.
**電話する** (denwa suru) = 'to make a phone call': **電話** (denwa, telephone) + **する**. Direction: **〜に電話する** = 'call [person]'.

sentences.base_language: My mother answers and she sounds happy.
sentences.target_language: お母さんが出て、嬉しそうな声でした。
sentences.romanized: Okāsan ga dete, ureshisō na koe deshita.
sentences.literal: Mother [subj] answers and happy-seeming voice was.
sentences.grammarNote:
**出る** (deru) = 'to answer (the phone) / to pick up': **電話に出る** (denwa ni deru, answer the phone). **出て** is the te-form connecting to the next clause.
**嬉しそうな声** (ureshisō na koe) = 'a voice that sounded happy': **嬉しい** (ureshii, happy) → **嬉しそう** (looks/sounds happy) + **な** (na-adj connector) + **声** (koe, voice).
**〜そうな** modifies the noun that follows: 'a happy-seeming [voice/appearance]'. The **な** is required because **〜そう** functions as a な-adjective.

sentences.base_language: I tell her I am feeling lonely.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と伝える** (to tsutaeru) = 'to convey that ~ / to let [someone] know that ~': the **と** is the quotation particle marking what was conveyed.
**伝える** (tsutaeru) = 'to convey / pass on [a message]': implies the communication successfully reaches the recipient, unlike **言う** (iu, to say) which just describes speech.
**寂しい** (sabishii) = 'lonely' (い-adjective). In direct/indirect speech before **と**, adjectives use the plain form: 寂しい (not 寂しいです).

sentences.base_language: My mother says, 'You are doing your best.'
sentences.target_language: 「頑張っているね」と言います。
sentences.grammarNote: ね (ne) is a sentence-ending particle that seeks agreement or shares a feeling with the listener: 寒いですね (it's cold, isn't it). Unlike よ (yo) which asserts new information, ね invites the listener to feel the same way — here it's empathetic and warm.

sentences.base_language: She tells me she will cook my favourite dish when I come home.
sentences.target_language: 帰ったら好きな料理を作ってくれると言います。
sentences.romanized: Kaettara suki na ryōri wo tsukutte kureru to iimasu.
sentences.literal: When-return favourite dish [obj] make give-me [quote] says.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜たら (〜tara) is a conditional meaning 'when/if ~ happens': 帰ったら (when I return home). It is used when the condition is certain to happen eventually, often for future plans. Compare 〜ば which is more hypothetical.

sentences.base_language: After the call I feel much better.
sentences.target_language: 電話の後、ずっと気持ちが楽になりました。
sentences.romanized: Denwa no ato, zutto kimochi ga raku ni narimashita.
sentences.literal: Phone-call after, much feeling [subj] easier became.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜の後** (no ato) = 'after ~': **電話の後** (after the phone call). Compare **〜てから** (te kara, after doing ~) which connects actions: **電話してから** (after making the phone call).
**ずっと** (zutto) here means 'much / a great deal' in the sense of degree change — 'became much easier'. It can also mean 'all along / continuously' depending on context.
**気持ちが楽になる** = 'to feel relieved / lighter': **楽** (raku, easy/comfortable) + **になる** (ni naru, become). The pattern **[い-adj stem]くなる** or **[な-adj]になる** expresses change of state.

sentences.base_language: I feel grateful that I have my family.
sentences.target_language: 家族がいることに感謝しています。
sentences.romanized: Kazoku ga iru koto ni kansha shite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことに感謝する** (koto ni kansha suru) = 'to be grateful for the fact that ~': **こと** nominalizes the clause **家族がいる** (my family exists/is here) into a noun phrase.
**感謝する** (kansha suru) = 'to be grateful / give thanks': **感** (kan, feeling) + **謝** (sha, gratitude/apology). The **に** marks what the gratitude is directed at.
**家族がいる** uses **いる** (iru, exist/be) for animate beings — people and animals. Compare **ある** (aru) for inanimate objects and abstract things.
